us-politics.headline: US Warplanes Shot Down Over Iran, Crew Member Missing
us-politics.body:
Two U.S. warplanes were shot down during operations over Iran, with at least one crew member still missing. Two helicopters were also hit during recovery efforts, raising the military and political stakes of a conflict that has drawn no formal declaration of war from Congress. The losses mark a serious escalation.

Republican Senator John Curtis called for ending offensive operations without congressional authorization, while Rep. Nancy Mace voiced concern about further escalation and ground troops. NPR notes Trump's wartime rhetoric diverges sharply from past presidents, with critics arguing the framing obscures accountability. Democrats slammed prediction market Polymarket for taking bets on the missing pilot's fate.

sources.title: What to know about US fighter jets hit in Iran war
2 US helicopters hit during recovery efforts in Iran
GOP senator calls to end Iran operations without formal declaration of war
Mace ‘concerned about escalation’ in Iran war
Democrat rips Polymarket for taking bets on fate of US pilot shot down over Iran
How Trump's wartime rhetoric differs from past presidents and why that matters
President Trump's rocky week at home and abroad

us-politics.headline: Trump Budget Draws GOP Dissent, Reshapes DOJ Leadership
us-politics.body:
Trump's budget proposal cuts TSA funding, mandates privatization at small airports, and redirects $10 billion toward Washington D.C. construction projects. Republican Senator Susan Collins publicly criticized the funding cuts, a rare break from her party's leadership. The defense budget outline drew scrutiny over its priorities even as the Iran conflict intensifies.

Todd Blanche stepped into the attorney general role following Pam Bondi's departure, carrying both Trump's expectations and the weight of his prior role as Trump's personal lawyer. Blanche told reporters there is no evidence in the Epstein files that Epstein worked as a spy. Senator Thom Tillis separately set a Jan. 6 red line for any future attorney general nominee.

sources.title: Todd Blanche takes on DOJ’s top job and the burden of Trump’s expectations
Susan Collins knocks funding cuts in White House budget proposal
What to know about Trump’s defense budget proposal
Blanche: No evidence in Epstein files he was a spy
Trump proposes $10B fund for D.C. construction, beautification projects
Trump budget would cut TSA funding, require privatization at small airports

us-politics.headline: States Sue Over Mail-In Ballot Order as Voting Fight Widens
us-politics.body:
A coalition of Democratic-led states filed suit against Trump's executive order restricting mail-in voting, with Oregon's attorney general leading a separate challenge. Oregon officials described the order's effects as creating "confusion, chaos and distrust" in election administration. The legal battles are expected to reach federal courts quickly.

The order targets procedures that Democrats argue are well-established and legally protected. Republicans contend tighter controls are necessary to ensure election integrity. The fight adds to a broader pattern of states clashing with the White House over federal authority in elections.

sources.title: ‘Confusion, chaos and distrust’: Oregon challenges Trump’s order restricting vote-by-mail
Democratic-led states sue over Trump mail-in ballot order

us-politics.headline: Trump Signs College Sports Order, Praises God on Good Friday
us-politics.body:
Trump signed an executive order aimed at reforming college sports, focusing on issues including athlete compensation and NCAA governance. The move follows earlier directives on the same subject and signals continued White House interest in reshaping amateur athletics. Critics question whether executive action is the right tool for what is largely a private regulatory dispute.

Separately, Trump issued a Good Friday message declaring "America needs God" and pointing to what he called a "resurgence of religion" in the country. The statement drew praise from conservative commentators and fits a pattern of Trump aligning his presidency with religious themes. No formal policy accompanied the message.

sources.title: Trump signs order aimed at reforming college sports
Trump says ‘America needs God’ in Good Friday message touting ‘resurgence of religion'
Trump again orders changes to ‘save’ college sports

au-politics.headline: Drug bust, fuel fears, and farm pay disputes grip Australia
au-politics.body:
Nine people face charges after a sunken trawler off Victoria exposed an alleged plot to import tonnes of cocaine and methamphetamine via a 'mother ship' in Australian waters. Police say the syndicate was sophisticated and well-resourced. The bust is one of the largest alleged drug importation conspiracies in recent memory.

Meanwhile, fuel demand remains elevated despite no confirmed supply shortage, as consumer anxiety keeps bowsers busy. Farmers are adding to economic pressure, urging major supermarkets to lift prices paid for fresh produce - arguing current rates are unsustainable and threaten domestic food supply.

sources.title: Fuel demand stays high, as farmers urge supermarkets to pay more for fresh produce
Nine charged over alleged conspiracy to import tonnes of cocaine and meth via ‘mother ship’ in Australian waters
Sunken trawler alerts police to alleged drug-smuggling plan

au-politics.headline: Iran shoots down US jet as Russia strikes Ukraine on Good Friday
au-politics.body:
Iran has downed a US aircraft, an incident analysts say will shock Americans on multiple fronts - strategically, politically, and in terms of public confidence in the Trump administration's foreign policy. The episode risks drawing Washington into a confrontation it has so far tried to avoid.

Separately, Russia launched daytime strikes on Ukraine on Good Friday, killing at least 14 people including in residential areas of Kyiv. The attacks drew international condemnation. Both incidents land amid ongoing uncertainty about US engagement in global security commitments.

sources.title: Russia launches Good Friday daytime attacks on Ukraine, killing 14
Iran's downing of a US jet will shock Americans in three ways

au-politics.headline: Data centres spark blackout and housing fears in Sydney councils
au-politics.body:
Sydney councils are raising alarms over a wave of proposed data centres, warning they could strain the electricity grid to the point of causing local blackouts. Councils also argue the facilities consume land earmarked for housing at a time when Sydney faces acute supply pressure.

Health concerns are also on the table, with residents near proposed sites worried about noise, heat output, and air quality. Proponents say data infrastructure is essential for Australia's digital economy. The tension reflects a broader national debate about how to balance technology investment against community and planning needs.

sources.title: Sydney councils fear new datacentres could cause blackouts, block housing and affect locals’ health

au-politics.headline: Artemis II heads moonward as space's future sparks debate
au-politics.body:
NASA's Artemis II crew is en route to the Moon, releasing what the agency calls extraordinary images of Earth from deep space. The mission marks the first crewed lunar journey since Apollo, with notable upgrades including onboard toilets and reused rocket components.

The mission is not without controversy. Commentators on the right question whether Elon Musk's growing influence over US space infrastructure - through SpaceX contracts central to Artemis - represents a healthy public-private partnership or an uncomfortable concentration of power in a single commercial operator.

sources.title: Artemis foul? The future of space has a distinct Musk about it
'Extraordinary' images of home as astronauts head towards Moon
This moon mission is different. They have toilets now

world.headline: US-Iran War Escalates as Hormuz Tensions Grip Region
world.body:
Iran shot down two US warplanes and rescue teams are conducting what officials describe as a harrowing search for at least one missing American crew member inside Iranian territory. Israel simultaneously struck bridges in Lebanon, widening the conflict's footprint across the Middle East. The WHO reports more than 20 attacks on Iranian healthcare facilities since March 1.

The Strait of Hormuz remains a flashpoint. A French-owned vessel became one of the first commercial ships to pass through the waterway amid intense international pressure to keep it open. Separately, an intercepted attack on a UAE gas facility at Habshan killed at least one person, signaling the war's reach into Gulf infrastructure. Survivors of an earlier US-Israeli bridge strike inside Iran gave harrowing accounts of the assault.

sources.title: Rescue team in Iran face 'harrowing and dangerous' search for US crew member
Iran war live: Tehran downs 2 US warplanes; Israel bombs Lebanon bridges
Will force be used to reopen Strait of Hormuz?
Survivors of Iran bridge attack recall terror of US-Israeli strike
More than 20 attacks on Iranian healthcare facilities since March 1: WHO
At least one killed at UAE’s Habshan gas facility after intercepted attack
French-owned ship passes through Strait of Hormuz
What we know about the US fighter jet shot down in Iran

world.headline: Gaza and Lebanon Christians Mark Good Friday Under Fire
world.body:
Palestinian Christians in Gaza observed Good Friday as Israeli military operations continued across the territory. In Lebanon, Christian communities held services under the shadow of ongoing Israeli strikes, with monks conducting Easter prayers in a largely deserted Old City of Jerusalem. The scenes underscored how the broader regional conflict has disrupted civilian and religious life across multiple countries simultaneously.

The contrast was stark - ancient religious rituals playing out against active bombardment. Both Gaza and Lebanon communities pressed ahead with observances despite the danger.

sources.title: Palestinian Christians in Gaza mark Good Friday
Christians in Lebanon observe Good Friday under Israeli attacks
Monks hold Easter prayers in deserted Old City of Jerusalem

world.headline: Cuba Frees 2,000 Prisoners as US Pressure Mounts
world.body:
Cuba has begun releasing more than 2,000 prisoners in a move that follows sustained pressure from Washington. The releases represent one of the largest such actions by Havana in recent years and come as the US maintains a hardline posture toward the island's government. Cuban authorities have not publicly framed the releases as a concession.

The timing is significant. With the US simultaneously engaged in a war with Iran and facing domestic legal battles - including two dozen Democrat-led states suing the Trump administration over mail-in ballot restrictions - the Cuba move adds another pressure point to an already stretched foreign policy agenda.

sources.title: Cuba begins releasing more than 2,000 prisoners as US pressure mounts
Cuba releases over 2,000 prisoners amid mounting US pressure
Two dozen Democrat-led states sue Trump over mail-in ballot limits

world.headline: Sudan Hospital Bombed; Artemis II Crew Captures Earth From Space
world.body:
A drone strike on a hospital in Sudan killed at least 10 people, according to medical charity MSF. The attack is the latest in a brutal conflict that has repeatedly targeted civilian infrastructure. Sudan's war has largely fallen off the international agenda despite a mounting humanitarian toll.

On a sharply different note, NASA's Artemis II crew released the first photographs of Earth taken from the moon mission's trajectory - images described as spectacular. The mission marks the first crewed lunar flight in over five decades, offering a rare moment of international scientific optimism amid a week dominated by conflict.

sources.title: Artemis II crew take 'spectacular' image of Earth
NASA releases stunning first photos of Earth from Artemis II moon mission
Drone strike on Sudan hospital kills 10, medical charity MSF says

ai.headline: Anthropic Dominates Headlines With Acquisition, PAC, and Claude Restrictions
ai.body:
Anthropic is moving fast on multiple fronts. The company reportedly acquired biotech startup Coefficient Bio for $400 million, launched a political action committee, and is drawing heavy investor interest in private markets - even as SpaceX's anticipated fundraising threatens to pull capital away.

On the product side, Anthropic quietly cut off OpenClaw, a popular third-party Claude client, by requiring subscribers to pay extra to use it through the API. The move effectively bans the tool for most users. OpenClaw also faces separate scrutiny after a security researcher warned its users to assume their accounts have been compromised following a data breach.

sources.title: Anthropic is having a moment in the private markets; SpaceX could spoil the party
Anthropic essentially bans OpenClaw from Claude by making subscribers pay extra
Tell HN: Anthropic no longer allowing Claude Code subscriptions to use OpenClaw
OpenClaw gives users yet another reason to be freaked out about security
Anthropic buys biotech startup Coefficient Bio in $400M deal: Reports
Anthropic ramps up its political activities with a new PAC

ai.headline: OpenAI Reshuffles Executives as AGI Lead Steps Away
ai.body:
OpenAI is reorganizing at the top. COO Brad Lightcap is moving into a new "special projects" role, while Fidji Simo is taking medical leave shortly after joining the company. The head of OpenAI's AGI division is also stepping back temporarily.

The simultaneous departures and role changes raise questions about internal stability at a company already navigating a high-stakes transition from nonprofit to for-profit structure. No successors have been named publicly.

sources.title: OpenAI executive shuffle includes new role for COO Brad Lightcap to lead ‘special projects’
OpenAI’s AGI boss is taking a leave of absence
OpenAI’s Fidji Simo Is Taking Medical Leave Amid an Executive Shake-Up

ai.headline: Musk Ties SpaceX IPO Banks to Grok Subscriptions
ai.body:
Elon Musk is reportedly requiring banks competing for SpaceX's IPO mandate to purchase Grok subscriptions - effectively using one of the most coveted deals in finance as leverage to drive revenue for his AI venture xAI. The demand puts banks in an awkward position, forcing them to weigh compliance against the optics of paying for access.

Meanwhile, Trump's broader AI ambitions are stumbling. His own tariffs are driving up hardware costs and slowing the data center buildout he promised, while public opposition to new facilities is growing - surveys show Americans prefer Amazon warehouses to data centers in their neighborhoods.

sources.title: Elon Musk insists banks working on SpaceX IPO must buy Grok subscriptions
Trump ignores biggest reasons his AI data center buildout is failing
People would rather have an Amazon warehouse in their backyard than a data center

ai.headline: AI Erodes Critical Thinking as Iran Strikes Hit AWS
ai.body:
New research finds that heavy AI use is producing what scientists call "cognitive surrender" - users increasingly defer to model outputs without applying their own judgment, even when answers are wrong. The pattern holds across skill levels and appears to worsen with repeated use.

Separately, Iranian strikes on infrastructure in Bahrain and Dubai knocked Amazon Web Services availability zones "hard down," disrupting cloud services across the region. The outage highlights the physical vulnerability of the data centers underpinning AI and cloud computing - a risk that companies building new natural gas-powered facilities are only beginning to reckon with.

sources.title: Iran strikes leave Amazon availability zones "hard down" in Bahrain and Dubai
"Cognitive surrender" leads AI users to abandon logical thinking, research finds
AI companies are building huge natural gas plants to power data centers. What could go wrong?

btc.headline: Circle Faces Heat Over $285M Hack and Compliance Failures
btc.body:
Circle is under serious pressure after refusing to freeze USDC stolen in a $285 million hack of Drift, a Solana-based trading platform. Critics argue Circle's inaction undermines one of the core selling points of regulated stablecoins - the ability to intervene when funds are clearly stolen.

On-chain investigator ZachXBT piled on separately, accusing Circle of $420 million in compliance failures since 2022, alleging the company repeatedly allowed illicit funds to flow through its network. Together, the two stories paint a damaging picture of a company preparing for a public listing while struggling to defend its compliance record.

The timing is brutal. Circle's IPO ambitions make regulatory scrutiny far more consequential now than at any previous point in the company's history.

sources.title: Circle under fire after $285 million Drift hack over inaction to freeze stolen USDC
ZachXBT accuses Circle of $420M in 'compliance failures' since 2022

btc.headline: Schwab Enters Spot Crypto Trading as Bitcoin Dips Below $70K
btc.body:
Charles Schwab confirmed plans to launch direct spot trading for Bitcoin and Ether in the first half of 2026, a move that brings one of America's largest retail brokerages fully into the crypto market. The announcement signals that institutional on-ramps are widening even as prices soften.

Bitcoin slipped below $70,000 this week, and corporate treasury strategies are starting to diverge under the pressure. Some firms are holding firm while others are reassessing their BTC positions, exposing a fault line between conviction holders and opportunistic adopters.

A former hedge fund manager also raised the question of whether Bitcoin is underpricing geopolitical risk tied to a prolonged Iran conflict - a macro variable most crypto traders have largely ignored.

sources.title: Schwab plans spot bitcoin, ether trading launch in first half of 2026
Crypto Biz: Bitcoin treasuries break ranks as BTC dips below $70K
Is Bitcoin mispricing a prolonged Iran war? Ex-hedge fund manager weighs in

btc.headline: Quantum Threat to Bitcoin Is Real but Not Imminent
btc.body:
A widely circulated claim that quantum computers could crack Bitcoin in nine minutes sparked alarm this week, but the reality is more nuanced. Current quantum hardware cannot break Bitcoin's elliptic curve cryptography at any meaningful scale - the nine-minute figure applies to theoretical future machines operating at millions of error-corrected qubits, far beyond what exists today.

The story matters because it is forcing a sharper public conversation about post-quantum cryptography and how long the industry has to prepare. Bitcoin developers have been aware of the long-term risk for years, but no upgrade path has been formally adopted. The window to act is open - for now.

sources.title: Here's what 'cracking' bitcoin in 9 minutes by quantum computers actually means

btc.headline: Coinbase Trust Charter Draws Fire From Community Banks
btc.body:
US community banks are pushing back against the OCC's move to grant Coinbase a federal trust charter, arguing it gives the crypto exchange a regulatory foothold that bypasses the standards traditional banks must meet. The opposition reflects a broader tension between legacy financial institutions and crypto firms gaining direct access to federal oversight frameworks.

Elsewhere in regulation, Cambodia proposed stiff prison sentences for crypto scammers as Southeast Asian governments tighten enforcement. Meanwhile, the Ethereum Foundation is close to hitting its 70,000 staked ETH target, a milestone that signals growing internal commitment to the network's proof-of-stake infrastructure.

sources.title: US community banks oppose OCC's approval of Coinbase trust charter
Cambodian lawmakers propose severe prison time for crypto scammers
Ethereum Foundation nearly reaches 70,000 staked ETH goal

food.name: Miso-Glazed Leek and Walnut Tart
food.description: A buttery shortcrust tart filled with slow-roasted leeks lacquered in white miso and honey, topped with toasted walnuts.
food.tip: Blind-bake the pastry shell for 12 minutes before adding the filling to prevent a soggy base — leeks release more moisture than you expect.

exercise.name: The April Ladder
exercise.equipment: A park bench or low wall, running shoes
exercise.description: Run 400m, do 10 step-ups, run 800m, do 20 step-ups, run 1200m, do 30 step-ups, then reverse the ladder back down — a structured outdoor session that builds both aerobic capacity and leg strength without a gym.

learn.description: Stochastic resonance is the counterintuitive phenomenon where adding a precise amount of random noise to a weak signal actually makes that signal easier to detect — it has been observed in neurons, climate systems, and even human sensory perception.
learn.why: It quietly upends the assumption that noise is always the enemy of clarity, and once you understand it you start seeing its fingerprints everywhere from how your ears process faint sounds to why financial markets sometimes behave more predictably during volatile periods.
